extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system supports **real-time data exchange capabilities**, ensuring continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts with **low-latency bidirectional communication**. The framework emphasizes **dynamic operational needs** over static BIM representations, implying temporal granularity aligned with operational monitoring (e.g., sub-second to minute-level updates for building control systems). Runtime configuration management enables **on-the-fly adjustments** to synchronization frequencies, adapting to varying operational requirements without full redeployment. Latency requirements are implicitly addressed through modular architecture and standardized extension points, ensuring scalability for time-sensitive services (e.g., HVAC, security, or energy management systems).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin framework adopts a **centralized yet modular architecture**, with a **single central hub** acting as the core orchestration layer for bidirectional data exchange. **Multiple Digital Twin instances** can be deployed per building or infrastructure component, each managed by distinct roles (e.g., Building Control Engineers for device modeling, Facility Managers for monitoring, and DT Engineers for service integration). Hierarchical structure is implied through the **BIM-to-DT life cycle flow**, where enriched BIM models serve as foundational inputs for operational DTs. Coordination mechanisms are facilitated via the framework’s **modular extension points**, enabling decentralized service integration while maintaining centralized control for system-wide consistency. Responsibilities are scoped by role: Building Control Engineers handle **device modeling and deployment**, Facility Managers interact with **real-time operational twins**, and DT Engineers extend capabilities via **custom services** without altering the core architecture.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system follows a **centralized hub-and-spoke architecture**, where the **DT Framework** acts as the primary orchestration layer coordinating roles (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) and system components. Orchestration is achieved through **bidirectional data exchange**, enabling holistic building life cycle management. Component integration leverages **modular architecture with standardized extension points**, allowing seamless addition of custom services and analytics. Resource management is implied via **runtime configuration management**, enabling dynamic adjustments to system behavior without redeployment. System-wide coordination is facilitated by the framework’s **requirements-driven approach**, ensuring interoperability across heterogeneous systems while maintaining scalability from individual components to entire infrastructure systems.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Horizontal integration is enabled through **standardized extension points** and **interoperability across heterogeneous systems**, addressing NF_X non-functional requirements. The framework supports integration with external systems via **bidirectional data exchange protocols** (implicitly assumed to align with AECO domain standards, though not explicitly named). Dependencies on external systems are managed through modular design, allowing **low-overhead updates** and **scalability** for enterprise-level integration. Integration patterns include **BIM-to-DT workflows** (enriched BIM models as inputs) and **service-oriented extensions** for Facility Managers and DT Engineers. While specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, IoT protocols) are not mentioned, the emphasis on **interoperability** suggests adherence to industry standards for data exchange in the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) domain.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system employs a **model-driven deployment framework** that integrates **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** as the foundational data source, augmented with **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles. The computational models include:
- **Geometric Models**: Derived from BIM, representing static building structures and spatial configurations.
- **Behavioral Models**: Dynamically defined by **Building Control Engineers** within the BIM environment to encapsulate **sensors, actuators, and controllers**, enabling real-time operational logic.
- **Analytical Models**: Generated via automated transformation of enriched BIM models into deployable Digital Twin artifacts, leveraging MDE to minimize manual intervention.

**Data Sources**:
- Primary: BIM models (static representations) enriched with **BCS (Building Control Systems) components** (sensors, actuators, controllers).
- Secondary: Real-time data streams from physical infrastructure for synchronization.

**Data Management Approaches**:
- **Model Extraction**: Structured representations are extracted from BIM via MDE principles, ensuring semantic consistency.
- **Runtime Synchronization**: Continuous bidirectional data exchange between physical buildings and their Digital Twin counterparts to maintain temporal coherence.
- **Modular Architecture**: Standardized extension points enable integration of **custom services and analytics**, facilitating evolution of the Digital Twin’s functionality.

**Model Relationships**:
- **Hierarchical**: BIM serves as the base layer, with behavioral and analytical models layered atop for dynamic control and simulation.
- **Interoperable**: Enables seamless interaction between **Facility Managers** (monitoring/control) and **DT Engineers** (extending functionality) via standardized interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The framework supports **physical actuators** and control mechanisms primarily through **Building Control Systems (BCS)** components defined within the BIM environment. Key characteristics include:
- **Types of Actuators**: Implicitly modeled as configurable elements within BIM, enabling integration of **HVAC systems, lighting controls, and automated shading mechanisms**.
- **Control Capabilities**: Defined by **Building Control Engineers** via logic rules embedded in the BIM model, allowing remote and automated actuation.
- **Remote Control Interfaces**: Facilitated through the Digital Twin’s interactive visualization layer, enabling **Facility Managers** to operate physical infrastructure remotely.
- **Actuation Ranges/Limitations**: Constrained by BIM-defined parameters (e.g., sensor thresholds, actuator response times) and physical device specifications.
- **Safety Constraints**: Implicitly enforced via BIM-integrated logic (e.g., fail-safe protocols for emergency systems).
- **Addressing Mechanisms**: Devices are addressed via **semantic identifiers** derived from the BIM model, ensuring unique and context-aware control.

**Technical Note**: The framework does not explicitly detail actuator hardware specifications (e.g., power ratings, response times) beyond their BIM-based modeling constraints.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The sensing infrastructure is modeled within the BIM environment as part of the **Building Control Systems (BCS)** components, with the following specifications:
- **Sensor Types**: Implicitly represented as **sensors** (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy, motion) integrated into the BIM model by **Building Control Engineers**.
- **Measurement Capabilities**: Defined by BIM parameters (e.g., range, units) but lacks explicit technical specs (e.g., resolution, calibration intervals).
- **Spatial Distribution**: Configurable within BIM to reflect physical placement in the building (e.g., floor-level, room-specific sensors).
- **Sampling Frequencies**: Not explicitly detailed in the documents; assumed to be configurable via BIM logic but not quantified.
- **Data Transmission Rates**: Synchronized via the framework’s **real-time data exchange capabilities**, though specific bandwidth or latency metrics are absent.
- **Accuracy/Precision Specifications**: Not provided; constrained by BIM-defined sensor models and underlying hardware (not detailed).
- **Data Collection Mechanisms**: Automated via the Digital Twin’s **runtime synchronization** layer, ensuring continuous data ingestion from physical sensors into the BIM-enriched model.

**Technical Note**: The framework abstracts sensor specifics, focusing instead on their **semantic integration** within the BIM-based Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin framework addresses **fidelity and validity** through a combination of **model-driven validation**, **runtime synchronization**, and **quality assurance processes**:
- **Model Fidelity Requirements**:
  - **BIM Enrichment**: Sensors, actuators, and controllers are modeled within BIM to bridge its static nature with dynamic Digital Twin requirements.
  - **Automated Artifact Generation**: MDE principles ensure deployable Digital Twin artifacts align with operational needs, reducing manual errors.

- **Validation and Verification Methods**:
  - **Pre-Deployment**: BIM models undergo **semantic validation** to ensure consistency in sensor/actuator definitions.
  - **Runtime**: Continuous **bidirectional synchronization** between physical and digital systems detects discrepancies (e.g., sensor drift, control logic mismatches).

- **Uncertainty Sources and Quantification**:
  - **Model Uncertainty**: Stemming from BIM’s static limitations (e.g., dynamic system behavior not fully captured).
  - **Data Uncertainty**: Introduced by real-time sensor noise or transmission delays (not quantified in documents).
  - **Quantification**: Implicit via **error detection mechanisms** in runtime synchronization (specific metrics not provided).

- **Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms**:
  - **Automated Alerts**: Triggered via the Digital Twin’s interactive layer for Facility Managers to address anomalies.
  - **Custom Services**: **DT Engineers** can develop plugins to enhance error correction (e.g., adaptive calibration algorithms).

- **Quality Assurance Processes**:
  - **Lifecycle Validation**: Spans from **device modeling** (BIM) to **custom service deployment**, ensuring end-to-end consistency.
  - **Modular Extensibility**: Standardized interfaces allow third-party validation tools to integrate for enhanced QA.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin (DT) framework enables **real-time bidirectional data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, leveraging **enriched BIM models** as the foundational data source. Key components include:

- **Data Types Transmitted**:
  - Building operational data (e.g., sensor readings: temperature, humidity, occupancy, energy consumption, structural vibrations, HVAC performance metrics).
  - Device-level telemetry (e.g., IoT sensor payloads, actuator statuses, equipment health indicators).
  - Contextual environmental data (e.g., weather conditions, indoor air quality metrics).

- **Communication Protocols**:
  - Standardized protocols for **device modeling and data ingestion**, though specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, BACnet) are implied for interoperability with heterogeneous systems (as per **NF_X interoperability requirements**).
  - **Bidirectional data exchange** facilitated by the DT Framework’s central hub, ensuring synchronization between physical and virtual domains.

- **Transmission Frequencies**:
  - **Continuous or event-triggered synchronization** to maintain real-time alignment, with no explicit frequency mentioned but inferred as **low-latency** (critical for operational decision-making).

- **Event Triggers**:
  - **Dynamic triggers** based on operational anomalies (e.g., sensor thresholds breached, equipment failures) or scheduled updates (e.g., daily/weekly maintenance cycles).
  - **Runtime configuration management** allows adaptive data ingestion policies without redeployment.

- **Data Quality Assurance**:
  - Implicit through the framework’s **requirements-driven approach**, ensuring **low-overhead updates** and **maintainability** to sustain data integrity.
  - Preprocessing steps likely include **validation, normalization, and enrichment** of BIM data to align with operational needs (e.g., converting static BIM geometry to dynamic DT attributes).

- **Data Integration Mechanisms**:
  - **Modular architecture** with **standardized extension points** enables pluggable data pipelines for custom preprocessing (e.g., filtering, aggregation, or transformation of raw sensor data).
  - Integration with **BIM tools** (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) for initial device modeling, followed by **runtime adjustments** via the DT Framework’s hub.

The framework explicitly bridges **BIM’s static representations** with **dynamic operational data**, addressing gaps in traditional building information modeling through **real-time synchronization** and **interactive visualization interfaces** for monitoring and control.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in this DT framework facilitates **control command execution** from the digital twin to physical building systems, governed by a **bidirectional, centralized hub architecture**. Key technical aspects include:

- **Command Types**:
  - **Operational controls**: HVAC adjustments, lighting automation, equipment start/stop commands.
  - **Maintenance directives**: Predictive maintenance alerts, calibration requests, or emergency shutdowns.
  - **Configuration updates**: Dynamic reconfiguration of building zones or system parameters (e.g., adjusting setpoints via the DT interface).

- **Control Message Formats**:
  - Standardized formats aligned with **interoperability requirements (NF_X)**, though explicit protocols (e.g., JSON, XML, or protocol-specific payloads like OPC UA) are not detailed.
  - Messages likely include **command payloads**, **metadata** (e.g., timestamp, source system), and **validation checksums** for integrity.

- **Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
  - **Runtime configuration management** ensures commands adhere to **operational constraints** (e.g., authority checks, parameter validations).
  - **Centralized hub** enforces **access control policies** for Facility Managers and DT Engineers, though explicit authentication protocols (e.g., OAuth, TLS) are not specified.

- **Feedback Systems**:
  - **Real-time feedback loops** confirm command execution status (e.g., acknowledgment from actuators, sensor confirmation of state changes).
  - **Conflict resolution** mechanisms are implied via the framework’s **modular architecture**, allowing dynamic rerouting or prioritization of commands (e.g., emergency overrides).

- **Command Execution Confirmation**:
  - **Bidirectional synchronization** ensures the DT reflects physical state changes post-execution, with **low-latency updates** to maintain consistency.
  - **Event-driven triggers** (e.g., sensor feedback) may auto-correct discrepancies or trigger alerts for unresolved conflicts.

The framework’s **holistic life cycle management** approach ensures commands are executed within the context of **building operational needs**, with **adaptability** via runtime adjustments to avoid full redeployment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **technical network infrastructure** for this DT system is designed to support **real-time, bidirectional communication** between physical devices, the DT hub, and external systems, adhering to **non-functional requirements (NF_X)** for interoperability, scalability, and reliability. Key specifications include:

- **Communication Protocols**:
  - **Standardized, heterogeneous protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, BACnet) for device connectivity, though exact implementations are not detailed.
  - **Modular architecture** enables **plug-and-play integration** of custom protocols via extension points.

- **Network Topology and Architecture**:
  - **Centralized hub architecture** with a **distributed edge component** for low-latency processing (implied by **runtime configuration management** and **scalability requirements**).
  - **Hierarchical topology**: Physical devices → Edge/on-premise gateways → Central DT hub → Cloud/edge services (if applicable).

- **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
  - **Low-latency** (<100ms, inferred from real-time synchronization needs) for critical control loops (e.g., HVAC, safety systems).
  - **Scalable bandwidth** to accommodate **entire infrastructure systems** (NF_X scalability requirement), though exact throughput (e.g., Mbps) is not specified.

- **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
  - **Redundant data paths** via the hub’s **modular design** to mitigate single points of failure.
  - **Runtime configuration adjustments** allow dynamic failover or load balancing without redeployment.

- **Security Protocols**:
  - **Encryption** (e.g., TLS) and **authentication** (e.g., role-based access control) implied for **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** (NF_X).
  - **Secure data exchange** between BIM tools, DT hub, and physical devices to prevent tampering or unauthorized access.

The infrastructure prioritizes **adaptability** and **maintainability**, enabling seamless integration of new devices or services while ensuring **continuous synchronization** between physical and digital domains.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **deployment infrastructure** for this DT system is designed with **flexibility, scalability, and operational resilience**, supporting **cloud, edge, and on-premise** deployment models to accommodate diverse AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) use cases. Key characteristics include:

- **Hosting Platforms**:
  - **Cloud-based**: For centralized analytics, large-scale data storage, and collaborative access (implied by **scalability requirements** for entire infrastructure systems).
  - **Edge computing**: Localized processing for low-latency control (e.g., building automation systems) to reduce cloud dependency.
  - **On-premise**: For organizations with strict data sovereignty or legacy system integration needs.

- **Computational and Storage Requirements**:
  - **Scalable compute resources** to handle **real-time data processing** and **enriched BIM model rendering** (e.g., GPU acceleration for visualization).
  - **Storage tiering**: High-performance storage for operational data (e.g., sensor telemetry) and archival storage for historical BIM/DT versions.

- **Scalability Mechanisms**:
  - **Modular architecture** with **standardized extension points** allows horizontal scaling of components (e.g., adding new sensor pipelines or analytics services).
  - **Runtime configuration management** enables **dynamic resource allocation** without redeployment, supporting **low-overhead updates**.

- **Deployment Models Supported**:
  - **Hybrid cloud-edge-on-premise**: Seamless integration across deployment models via the **centralized DT hub**.
  - **Containerized deployments** (implied by modularity) for portability and isolated service execution.

- **Availability/Reliability Assurance**:
  - **High availability (HA)** via redundant network paths and **failover mechanisms** (e.g., automatic switchover to edge nodes if cloud connectivity is lost).
  - **Disaster recovery (DR)** strategies aligned with **NF_X maintainability**, ensuring minimal downtime for updates or failures.

The framework’s **requirements-driven approach** ensures the deployment infrastructure aligns with **operational needs**, bridging **BIM’s static models** with **dynamic DT capabilities** while prioritizing **accessibility** for users with varying technical expertise.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations) domain spans multiple phases, including **conceptualization, design, construction, operation, and decommissioning**, with each phase featuring distinct representation types and transition management challenges. During **conceptualization**, DTs are modeled using **BIM (Building Information Modeling)** as the foundational digital representation, integrating parametric and geometric data to simulate asset behavior. In the **design phase**, DTs evolve into **high-fidelity digital prototypes** with embedded simulation models (e.g., structural, environmental, or operational simulations) to validate performance before physical construction. **Transition management** between design and construction phases relies on **data synchronization protocols** (e.g., IFC, COBie) to ensure consistency between BIM models and real-time sensor data from construction sites. The **operation phase** emphasizes **real-time synchronization** between physical assets and their digital counterparts, leveraging **sensor networks, IoT devices, and AI-driven analytics** to monitor performance, predict maintenance needs, and optimize lifecycle management. **Lifecycle governance** is fragmented in current implementations, lacking standardized frameworks; however, emerging approaches advocate for **unified engineering workflows** and **stakeholder alignment** to ensure seamless transitions across phases. Organizational barriers, such as **high implementation costs and proprietary ecosystems** (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js), further complicate lifecycle governance, necessitating **cross-disciplinary collaboration** and **modular, scalable architectures** for long-term sustainability.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process in AECO Digital Twins involves **methodologies rooted in BIM integration, workflow automation, and bidirectional communication** between physical and digital assets. Key methodologies include **parametric modeling** (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) for initial DT representation, **sensor-based data acquisition** (e.g., LiDAR, IoT sensors) for real-time updates, and **AI-driven analytics** (e.g., machine learning for anomaly detection). **Quality assurance (QA)** practices emphasize **data validation protocols** (e.g., IFC schema compliance checks) and **cross-platform interoperability testing** to mitigate fragmentation. **Testing and validation** are conducted through **simulation-based verification** (e.g., structural load testing in digital twins) and **real-world calibration** (e.g., comparing sensor data with BIM models). **Version control** is critical but often ad-hoc, with frameworks like **Eclipse Ditto** and **BaSyx** offering partial solutions through **API-based data versioning** and **event-driven synchronization**. **Evolution management** requires **modular design principles** to accommodate updates (e.g., adding new sensor data streams or AI models) without disrupting existing workflows. However, current implementations suffer from **manual configuration overhead** and **lack of out-of-the-box functionality**, particularly in AECO-specific contexts, where proprietary tools (e.g., iTwin.js) dominate but require substantial **manual effort** for integration.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Digital Twin systems in AECO generate **real-time and predictive insights** through **analytics and processing capabilities** rooted in **BIM, IoT, and AI integration**. Key insights include **operational performance metrics** (e.g., energy consumption, structural integrity), **predictive maintenance alerts** (via machine learning models trained on sensor data), and **cost modeling projections** (e.g., lifecycle cost analysis). **Decision-making processes** are supported by **interactive dashboards** (e.g., iTwin’s visualization tools) and **automated workflows** (e.g., triggering maintenance actions based on anomaly detection). **User interaction methods** range from **BIM-centric interfaces** (e.g., Revit plugins) to **cloud-based portals** (e.g., Autodesk Forge) for cross-stakeholder collaboration. **Confidence measures** for recommendations are derived from **data fidelity assessments** (e.g., sensor accuracy validation) and **simulation confidence intervals**, though current systems lack **unified confidence frameworks** due to fragmented data sources. Decision support is further constrained by **organizational silos** and **proprietary data formats**, limiting the scalability of insights across asset lifecycles.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Standardization in Digital Twin systems for AECO is governed by **industry-specific and interoperability standards**, though adoption remains inconsistent. **Relevant industry standards** include **ISO 19650 (Information Management for the Built Environment)**, which defines **BIM collaboration protocols**, and **IFC (Industry Foundation Classes)** for **data exchange between BIM tools**. **Specifications** like **COBie (Construction Operations Building Information Exchange)** are used for **asset handover and maintenance documentation**. **Certification requirements** are minimal but emerging, with frameworks like **BIM Level 2** mandating **IFC compliance** for public sector projects in the UK. **Compliance frameworks** (e.g., **OpenBIM**) promote **neutral data formats** but face challenges due to **vendor lock-in** (e.g., proprietary APIs in iTwin.js). **Interoperability standards** adopted include **OPC UA** for **industrial IoT integration** and **RESTful APIs** for **cloud-based DT connectivity**, though **bidirectional communication protocols** remain underdeveloped. Open-source frameworks (e.g., Eclipse Ditto, BaSyx) offer **standard compliance** (e.g., **OPC UA, MQTT**) but require **manual configuration**, exacerbating fragmentation in AECO deployments.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The provided documents emphasize **semantic coherence** and **runtime adaptability** as critical considerations for ensuring the integrity and safety of Digital Twin (DT) systems, particularly in Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) contexts. **Abbasi et al. [49]** highlight the phenomenon of *semantic drift*—where misalignment between digital and physical models poses risks to system reliability—mandating **synchronization techniques** to mitigate divergence and maintain model accuracy. **Kamburjan et al. [50]** propose **declarative lifecycle specification languages** to enforce rule-based management of DT instances, reducing operational risks through automated compliance checks. While explicit cybersecurity protocols (e.g., encryption, access control) or regulatory compliance frameworks (e.g., GDPR, ISO 27001) are not detailed, the documents imply that **tool interoperability** (as per Lehner et al. [48]) and **formalized governance models** (via MDE) underpin broader safety considerations by embedding organizational policies into DT architectures. Fail-safe mechanisms are indirectly addressed through **real-time data utilization** (e.g., Gerber et al. [33]) and **automated monitoring** for performance analysis, though no specific threat mitigation strategies (e.g., zero-trust architectures, intrusion detection) are explicitly mentioned.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],
block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.
block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
